region_of_origin: China (Palace cuisine tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Chinese citrus-flavoured shortcrust pastry. FOODON_03315091 (pastry food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW (Pastries and cakes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Chinese citrus shortcrust pastry in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Chinese pastry dessert.

main_ingredients: lotus paste or red bean paste (filling)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 180 g total, roughly 30 g per piece), as sold by the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Tart / Chinese Pastry (shortcrust type) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented by NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic Chinese pastry values
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Citrus Crisp' as a named product. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for Chinese shortcrust pastry (egg tart / wife cake analogues) with citrus flavouring. The food is a baked wheat-flour pastry with sweet filling, comparable in macronutrient profile to Chinese shortcrust pastry items. Energy and macros are cross-referenced against NCC generic pastry category anchors. All values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pastry and Sweet Baked Goods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate from shortcrust pastry / sweet biscuit category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Citrus Crisp. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for shortcrust pastry and sweet filled Chinese pastry analogues (GI range 60–70 reported for similar wheat-flour shortcrust baked goods with sweet filling). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 52.0 × 1.80) / 100 = 61.2. Serving size used: 180 g (6 pieces × 30 g each).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg/100 g) and moderate energy density
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces per occasion, as the cumulative sodium from a full 6-piece serving (~324 mg) contributes meaningfully to the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content (~52 g carbohydrate, ~22 g sugar per 100 g) with medium GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Citrus Crisp as an occasional indulgence only; the medium GI combined with high sugar load produces a substantial glycemic load per serving (~61 GL units for 6 pieces), requiring careful carbohydrate budgeting and post-meal glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from shortening or lard
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Citrus Crisp due to its saturated fat content from lard or shortening, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; a single serving of 6 pieces provides approximately 13.5 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit for high-risk individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Citrus Crisp (柑橘酥, Gān jú sū) is a traditional Chinese palace-style pastry dessert served as 6 pieces per order. It is crafted to resemble a miniature citrus fruit in appearance, reflecting the artisanal moulding techniques of imperial Chinese confectionery. The pastry shell is made from a wheat-flour shortcrust dough enriched with lard or vegetable shortening and egg yolk, producing a distinctively layered, crispy exterior that gives way to a soft, sweet filling — typically lotus paste or red bean paste infused with citrus zest or natural citrus flavouring. The combination of crispy and soft textures is a defining characteristic. The flavour profile is sweet with a bright, fragrant citrus note. Nutritionally, Citrus Crisp is an energy-dense dessert (~390 kcal/100 g) with moderate fat (~18 g/100 g, including ~7.5 g saturated fat) and high refined carbohydrate content (~52 g/100 g, ~22 g sugar). The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load per 6-piece serving. Clinically, this dessert warrants caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It contains no preservatives as stated by the restaurant. Allergens include gluten (wheat) and eggs. It is not certified halal. In Singapore, it is served at Chinese contemporary restaurants such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局).
common_user_queries: Is Citrus Crisp suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Citrus Crisp?
What is the GI of Chinese pastry?
What does Citrus Crisp taste like?
Where can I find Citrus Crisp in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 'Citrus Crisp' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for analogous Chinese shortcrust pastry products (egg tart, wife cake) and cross-referenced with NCC generic pastry category anchors. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for shortcrust pastry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogues for Chinese shortcrust pastry; no exact match exists in any approved primary source for 'Citrus Crisp' as a named product. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous USDA SR Legacy source entries used. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source. 9. Halal status is unconfirmed; lard is a traditional ingredient in Chinese shortcrust pastry and the restaurant has not published halal certification. 10. Trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from possible use of partially hydrogenated shortening; may be zero if pure lard or non-hydrogenated shortening is used. 11. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this product is not available. 12. GL per serving = (65 × 52.0 × 1.80) / 100 = 61.2, using 180 g serving (6 × 30 g pieces). 13. The description states 'without any preservatives' — this is preserved from the input source as a restaurant claim, not independently verified.
